When you click the Knit button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)

## Load relevant libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
## Simulate Genetic Data for Linear Regression
### Define Parameters and Simulate Data
### Number of iterations
Iters <- 1

### Define a matrix to store output summary statistics
mat <- matrix(rep(0, 3 * Iters), nrow = Iters, ncol = 3)

### Effect allele frequency
q <- 0.3

### Number of observations
N <- 1000

### Complementary allele frequency
p <- 1 - q

### Effect size for heterozygotes (assume additive model for now)
B1 <- 1.25

### Define allele counts from frequencies based on Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium (HWE)
### What if we didn't round?
Nrr <- round(p^2 * N)
Nra <- round(2 * p * q * N)
Naa <- N - Nrr - Nra

### Define genotype vectors
Xrr <- rep(0, Nrr)
Xra <- rep(1, Nra)
Xaa <- rep(2, Naa)
X <- c(Xrr, Xra, Xaa)

## Simulate Phenotypes and Perform Linear Regression

for (i in 1:Iters) {
  ### Simulate phenotypes (change lm to glm for binary phenotypes)
  Y <- (B1 * X) + rnorm(N, 10, 4) #note that we apply 'noise' here using arbitrary parameters
  
  ### Fit linear model
  model <- lm(Y ~ X)
  
  ### Extract p-value and effect size. We will play with these in a later demo.
  p <- summary(model)$coefficients[2, 4]
  B <- summary(model)$coefficients[2, 1]
  
  ### Store iteration number, p-value, and effect size
  mat[i, 1] <- i
  mat[i, 2] <- p
  mat[i, 3] <- B
}

### Convert matrix to data frame and name columns
mat <- as.data.frame(mat)
colnames(mat) <- c("iter", "p", "B")
mat
##   iter            p         B
## 1    1 3.703007e-06 0.9053774
## Summary Statistics and Plots

### Summary of genotype vector counts
table(X)
## X
##   0   1   2 
## 490 420  90
### Summary of phenotype vector counts
hist(Y)

### Display summary statistics of the last model fit
summary(model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Y ~ X)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -14.8326  -2.5928  -0.0763   2.4981  14.7709 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  10.1171     0.1718  58.879  < 2e-16 ***
## X             0.9054     0.1946   4.653  3.7e-06 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 3.987 on 998 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.02124,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.02026 
## F-statistic: 21.66 on 1 and 998 DF,  p-value: 3.703e-06
## The beta coefficient suggests that for each unit increase in the number of effect alleles (e.g., from 0 to 1, or 1 to 2), the expected change in the phenotype is Beta units. This implies a substantial effect size, where the presence of each additional effect allele is associated with an increase in the trait value by Beta units. The p-value indicates that the observed association is highly statistically significant. This means that there is a very low probability that the observed association is due to random chance. Typically, a p-value less than 0.05 is considered statistically significant, so a p-value this small provides strong evidence against the null hypothesis (i.e., no association between the genetic variant and the trait).

## Data Preparation for Plotting
### Prepare the output of a single iteration for plotting the data with style
Df <- as.data.frame(cbind(X, Y))
Df$X <- as.factor(Df$X) # What if we had included this earlier?

### Plotting with style
### Load geom_flat_violin function manually from the classroom github repo
#geom_flat_violin.R
# To do: Source the function from the GitHub URL
#https://gist.github.com/dgrtwo/eb7750e74997891d7c20#file-geom_flat_violin-r

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

"%||%" <- function(a, b) {
  if (!is.null(a)) a else b
}

geom_flat_violin <- function(mapping = NULL, data = NULL, stat = "ydensity",
                        position = "dodge", trim = TRUE, scale = "area",
                        show.legend = NA, inherit.aes = TRUE, ...) {
  layer(
    data = data,
    mapping = mapping,
    stat = stat,
    geom = GeomFlatViolin,
    position = position,
    show.legend = show.legend,
    inherit.aes = inherit.aes,
    params = list(
      trim = trim,
      scale = scale,
      ...
    )
  )
}

###' @rdname ggplot2-ggproto
###' @format NULL
###' @usage NULL
###' @export
GeomFlatViolin <-
  ggproto("GeomFlatViolin", Geom,
          setup_data = function(data, params) {
            data$width <- data$width %||%
              params$width %||% (resolution(data$x, FALSE) * 0.9)
            
            # ymin, ymax, xmin, and xmax define the bounding rectangle for each group
            data %>%
              group_by(group) %>%
              mutate(ymin = min(y),
                     ymax = max(y),
                     xmin = x,
                     xmax = x + width / 2)
          },
          
          draw_group = function(data, panel_scales, coord) {
            # Find the points for the line to go all the way around
            data <- transform(data, xminv = x,
                              xmaxv = x + violinwidth * (xmax - x))
            
            # Make sure it's sorted properly to draw the outline
            newdata <- rbind(plyr::arrange(transform(data, x = xminv), y),
                             plyr::arrange(transform(data, x = xmaxv), -y))
            
            # Close the polygon: set first and last point the same
            # Needed for coord_polar and such
            newdata <- rbind(newdata, newdata[1,])
            
            ggplot2:::ggname("geom_flat_violin", GeomPolygon$draw_panel(newdata, panel_scales, coord))
          },
          
          draw_key = draw_key_polygon,
          
          default_aes = aes(weight = 1, colour = "grey20", fill = "white", linewidth = 0.5,
                            alpha = NA, linetype = "solid"),
          
          required_aes = c("x", "y")
)

geom_flat_violin <- function(mapping = NULL, data = NULL, stat = "ydensity",
                             position = "dodge", trim = TRUE, scale = "area", 
                             show.legend = NA, inherit.aes = TRUE, ...) {
  ggplot2::layer(
    data = data,
    mapping = mapping,
    stat = stat,
    geom = GeomFlatViolin,
    position = position,
    show.legend = show.legend,
    inherit.aes = inherit.aes,
    params = list(trim = trim, scale = scale, ...)
  )
}

mixedPlot <- ggplot(Df, aes(X, Y, fill = X)) +
  geom_flat_violin(alpha = 0.5, position = position_nudge(x = .2, y = 0)) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.5, width = 0.15) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.5, width = .25, outlier.shape = NA) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

### Display the plot
mixedPlot

### Scatter plot of genotypes versus phenotypes
plot(X, Y)
